title: Sore throat
description: Evidence-aware home care and Australian escalation guidance for an acute sore throat.
audience: General readers and carers; not a diagnosis or individual treatment plan
summary: Most acute sore throats are caused by viruses and improve without antibiotics. Home care focuses on comfort, hydration and watching for signs that need medical assessment; symptoms alone cannot reliably establish the cause.

safety.emergencySummary: In Australia, call triple zero (000) for severe or life-threatening breathing difficulty. Seek medical help straight away for trouble breathing, severe pain, difficulty swallowing, neck stiffness, confusion or other signs of severe infection.

redFlags.text: Trouble breathing, severe pain, difficulty swallowing, neck stiffness, confusion, extreme tiredness or high fever needs urgent medical assessment.
A sore throat that persists, worsens, or occurs with fever, large tender neck lumps or new serious symptoms should be assessed by a doctor.

vulnerableGroups.text: People with a weakened immune system or another serious health condition should seek prompt medical advice when they develop a sore throat.

claims.statement: An uncomplicated acute sore throat is often self-limiting; many people improve within about one week, although symptoms may take up to about 10 days to settle.
claims.population: Adults and children with an uncomplicated acute sore throat

evidence.effectSummary: Most improve without antibiotics over approximately 7 to 10 days.
evidence.rationale: Consistent current guideline conclusions, but the exact course varies by cause, population and definition of recovery.

caveats: This course does not apply when symptoms are rapidly worsening, severe or associated with red flags.

claims.statement: Warm salt-water gargling is recommended by Australian consumer guidance as a comfort measure, but robust trial evidence for acute sore-throat benefit was not established in the reviewed evidence base.
claims.population: People old enough to gargle safely without swallowing the solution

evidence.effectSummary: May feel soothing; magnitude and reliability of benefit are uncertain.
evidence.rationale: Healthdirect recommends the practice for self-care, while NICE reported no qualifying evidence for mouthwashes in its acute sore-throat review. Guidance endorsement is not treated as proof of efficacy.

caveats: Do not use in anyone unable to gargle and spit safely.
This is comfort care, not treatment of the underlying cause.

practicalGuidance.title: Fluids and irritants
practicalGuidance.text: Drink enough fluid to avoid dehydration, rest, avoid smoke and other throat irritants, and avoid straining your voice. These measures support comfort and hydration; they are not presented as cures.

practicalGuidance.title: Pain medicines
practicalGuidance.text: Ask an Australian pharmacist or doctor whether a pain medicine is suitable for the person's age, health conditions, pregnancy or breastfeeding status, and other medicines. This draft does not provide dosing instructions.

practicalGuidance.title: Antibiotics
practicalGuidance.text: Do not use leftover or someone else’s antibiotics. Antibiotics do not treat viral respiratory infections; a clinician may recommend them for particular bacterial infections after assessment.

myths.correction: Most acute sore throats are viral or otherwise self-limiting. Antibiotics do not treat viruses and should be used only when a clinician determines they are appropriate.

jurisdictionNotes.text: For health advice in Australia, call healthdirect on 1800 022 222. Call triple zero (000) for a life-threatening emergency. Medicine selection should follow Australian labels and local pharmacist or clinician advice.

review.researchOwner: Codex-assisted draft; accountable human owner not yet assigned
review.changeSummary: Removed combined oral-analgesia and medicated-lozenge claims because directness, formulation and harms extraction were not adequate for claim-level review; corrected natural-history direction; retained the module as an unreviewed draft.
